nodes.name: React
nodes.rationale: React is a natural fit for Mastodon's complex, real-time social feed UI, where granular component-level re-rendering is critical for efficiently updating timelines, notifications, and streaming WebSocket data without full-page reloads. Its ecosystem (React Router for client-side navigation, Redux for predictable global state management of federated timelines and user sessions) forms a cohesive, well-supported stack that aligns with the scale and interactivity demands of a social network. The large React community also makes contributor onboarding easier for an open-source project with many external contributors.

alts.name: Vue.js
alts.blurb: Progressive component-based UI framework with gentler learning curve and built-in state/routing solutions
pros: Single File Components reduce context switching
Pinia/Vuex provide Redux-comparable state management
Smaller bundle size out of the box
More approachable for Rails developers familiar with ERB-style templating
cons: Smaller ecosystem of specialized social/real-time UI components vs React
Less mature TypeScript integration historically
Fewer experienced contributors likely in an open-source federated social context
alts.when: Choose Vue if the team is Rails-heavy and wants tighter, more opinionated tooling with less boilerplate than Redux

alts.name: Preact
alts.blurb: Lightweight React-compatible alternative with near-identical API but a 3KB runtime
pros: Drop-in React compatibility means minimal migration cost
Significantly smaller bundle improves load time for slower connections
Shares React's ecosystem via preact/compat shim
cons: Some React APIs (concurrent features, Suspense) lag behind or behave differently
Smaller community means fewer answers to edge-case bugs
Less corporate backing raises long-term maintenance risk
alts.when: Choose Preact if bundle size and initial load performance on low-bandwidth networks become a critical concern without wanting to rewrite component logic

alts.name: SolidJS
alts.blurb: Fine-grained reactivity framework with React-like JSX syntax but no virtual DOM
pros: Superior runtime performance for high-frequency streaming updates (ideal for live timelines)
No virtual DOM diffing overhead keeps feed rendering snappy
Smaller runtime bundle than React
cons: Much smaller ecosystem and community, harder open-source contributor onboarding
Incompatible with React libraries (Router, Redux, existing component libraries)
Full rewrite required; no migration path from existing React codebase
alts.when: Choose SolidJS only for a greenfield rewrite where streaming timeline render performance is the top priority and contributor pool can be assumed SolidJS-familiar

nodes.name: React Router
nodes.rationale: Mastodon's frontend is a complex single-page application built on React, and React Router is the de facto standard for declarative, component-based client-side routing in that ecosystem. It integrates naturally with the existing Redux state management and allows Mastodon's multi-column, timeline-heavy UI to manage deep navigation states without full-page reloads, which is critical for a real-time social platform. Its maturity and widespread adoption also means strong compatibility with Vite's build tooling and a large pool of contributors familiar with it.

alts.name: TanStack Router
alts.blurb: Type-safe, file-based or code-based router with first-class search param management and built-in data loading.
pros: Full TypeScript inference for routes and params
Built-in search param serialization, useful for Mastodon's filter/timeline states
Integrated loader pattern reduces Redux boilerplate for route-level data
cons: Younger ecosystem, fewer community examples
Migration from React Router v6 would require significant refactoring
Less familiar to most open-source contributors joining the project
alts.when: Choose this if the team prioritizes end-to-end type safety and wants to reduce Redux usage for route-driven data fetching.

alts.name: Wouter
alts.blurb: Minimalist ~2KB hook-based router API-compatible with basic React Router patterns.
pros: Tiny bundle footprint, beneficial for Mastodon's performance goals
Simple API reduces cognitive overhead for new contributors
Hook-first design aligns well with modern React patterns
cons: Lacks advanced features like nested layouts, data loaders, and scroll restoration
No built-in code-splitting support out of the box
Would require custom solutions for Mastodon's complex multi-column route nesting
alts.when: Choose this only if bundle size is the top priority and the routing needs are simple, which does not match Mastodon's complexity.

alts.name: Next.js App Router
alts.blurb: File-system-based routing baked into the Next.js full-stack React framework with SSR and RSC support.
pros: Server-side rendering improves initial load and SEO for public timelines
File-based routing reduces explicit route configuration
Built-in code splitting and image optimization
cons: Would require replacing or deeply integrating around the existing Rails/Puma backend
React Server Components conflict with the Redux-heavy, real-time client architecture
Enormous migration cost; essentially a rewrite of the frontend layer
alts.when: Consider only if starting a greenfield rewrite and willing to replace Rails as the primary web server with Next.js as the unified frontend/API layer.

nodes.name: Redux
nodes.rationale: Mastodon's frontend is a complex, real-time social media client managing deeply interconnected state — timelines, notifications, accounts, media, websocket streams — across many React components, making Redux's predictable centralized store and strict unidirectional data flow a natural fit. The large, established codebase benefits from Redux's mature ecosystem (Redux Toolkit, Redux Saga/Thunk) and excellent DevTools for debugging complex async flows like streaming timeline updates. Given the scale of the contributor base on an open-source project, Redux's explicit patterns and broad developer familiarity reduce onboarding friction.

alts.name: Zustand
alts.blurb: Lightweight, hook-based state library with minimal boilerplate and no provider wrapping required.
pros: Dramatically less boilerplate than Redux
Fine-grained subscriptions reduce unnecessary re-renders
Tiny bundle footprint (~1KB)
cons: No built-in DevTools as mature as Redux DevTools
Less prescriptive structure can lead to inconsistency in large teams
Async middleware patterns (sagas, thunks) must be handled manually
alts.when: Choose Zustand when starting a greenfield project or refactoring to reduce boilerplate, and the team is small enough to self-enforce architectural conventions.

alts.name: MobX
alts.blurb: Reactive, observable-based state management that automatically tracks dependencies and updates derived state.
pros: Minimal boilerplate with observable decorators or makeAutoObservable
Reactive derivations feel natural for feed/timeline data that transforms frequently
Good performance via fine-grained reactivity
cons: Implicit reactivity makes debugging and tracing state changes harder than Redux's explicit actions
Steeper mental model shift for teams already fluent in Redux
Smaller contributor mindshare in social/open-source React projects
alts.when: Choose MobX when your domain model is object-oriented and you want reactive derived state (e.g., computed timelines) without manual selector optimization.

alts.name: TanStack Query (React Query)
alts.blurb: Specialized server-state library handling fetching, caching, synchronization, and background refetching out of the box.
pros: Eliminates large swaths of Redux async boilerplate for API-driven data
Built-in cache invalidation, pagination, and optimistic updates
Pairs well with WebSocket subscriptions via manual cache updates
cons: Does not manage UI/client-local state (modals, compose window, settings) — still needs a complement
Requires rethinking existing Redux data-fetching patterns across a large codebase
Migration cost on a mature, multi-contributor OSS project is very high
alts.when: Choose TanStack Query (possibly alongside Zustand for local state) when building a new client or doing a major rewrite, where separating server state from UI state would simplify the architecture.

nodes.name: Rails
nodes.rationale: Rails is a natural fit for Mastodon's backend given its mature ecosystem for social-application concerns: Devise handles authentication, Doorkeeper provides OAuth2 (critical for ActivityPub client apps), and ActiveRecord pairs well with PostgreSQL for complex relational data like accounts, statuses, and follows. Rails' convention-over-configuration approach accelerates development of the REST/ActivityPub API surface, and its rich gem ecosystem (Sidekiq, Chewy/Elasticsearch) integrates seamlessly without custom glue code.

alts.name: Django
alts.blurb: Python's batteries-included web framework with comparable ORM, auth, and admin tooling
pros: Strong async support via ASGI/channels for real-time federation
Django REST Framework is well-matched to ActivityPub JSON-LD API needs
Large ecosystem with OAuth2 and Celery (Sidekiq equivalent) readily available
cons: No direct equivalents to Devise/Doorkeeper; more manual OAuth2 wiring
Python async story still maturing vs Ruby/Sidekiq background job maturity
Smaller pool of contributors familiar with both Django and ActivityPub
alts.when: Choose Django if your team is Python-first and you want native async support for high-concurrency federation traffic.

alts.name: Phoenix (Elixir)
alts.blurb: Elixir framework built on the BEAM VM, excelling at massive concurrency and real-time features
pros: Channels/LiveView handle WebSocket fan-out (streaming timelines) with far less infrastructure
BEAM's lightweight processes make high-volume ActivityPub delivery cheaper to scale
Pleroma/Akkoma prove this stack is viable for ActivityPub at production scale
cons: Much smaller gem/library ecosystem; Devise and Doorkeeper have no direct equivalents
Ecto differs enough from ActiveRecord that migrating existing schema is non-trivial
Smaller hiring pool and steeper learning curve for Rails-oriented contributors
alts.when: Choose Phoenix if horizontal scaling of real-time delivery and federation concurrency is the primary bottleneck and you can absorb ecosystem immaturity.

alts.name: Node.js + Express
alts.blurb: JavaScript runtime already partially present in the Mastodon stack for streaming
pros: Unifies language across frontend (React/Redux) and backend, reducing context switching
Express streaming server is already used in Mastodon for SSE; expanding it is consistent
Non-blocking I/O is well-suited to the high fan-out of ActivityPub delivery
cons: No mature equivalents to Devise/Doorkeeper; auth and OAuth2 require significant custom work
Lacks Rails' convention layer, meaning more boilerplate for complex relational data models
JavaScript's type safety and ORM maturity lag behind Rails + ActiveRecord for complex domain logic
alts.when: Choose Node.js if you are consolidating the stack to a single language and the team is JavaScript-heavy with appetite to build auth infrastructure from scratch.

nodes.name: Puma
nodes.rationale: Puma is the de facto standard multi-threaded app server for Rails applications and ships as the default in Rails itself, making it a natural fit for Mastodon's Rails backend. Its multi-process/multi-thread concurrency model handles Mastodon's high volume of concurrent HTTP and streaming connections efficiently, and it integrates seamlessly with Docker-based deployments where worker/thread counts can be tuned via environment variables. Mastodon's heavy use of background jobs via Sidekiq also means the web process can stay lean, playing directly to Puma's strengths.

alts.name: Unicorn
alts.blurb: Multi-process, single-threaded Ruby app server favored for simplicity and process isolation.
pros: True process isolation means a crash in one worker doesn't affect others
Zero copy-on-write memory efficiency with forking
Battle-tested in large Rails deployments (GitHub used it for years)
cons: No threading means far more processes needed to match Puma's concurrency, higher RAM usage
No built-in support for slow-client streaming connections common in ActivityPub
Effectively in maintenance mode; less active development
alts.when: Choose Unicorn if thread-safety of gems is a concern or you need strict process isolation and can afford the higher memory footprint per dyno/container.

alts.name: Falcon
alts.blurb: Async, fiber-based Ruby app server built on the async gem ecosystem for high concurrency.
pros: Non-blocking I/O via fibers enables very high concurrency with low thread count
Excellent fit for streaming and SSE endpoints Mastodon uses heavily
Rack-compatible, so Rails integration is straightforward
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem compared to Puma
Requires careful vetting that all dependencies are fiber-safe
Less operational tooling, documentation, and production case studies
alts.when: Choose Falcon if Mastodon's streaming API becomes a bottleneck and you want to explore async I/O without switching away from Ruby.

alts.name: Passenger (Phusion)
alts.blurb: Polyglot app server that embeds directly into Nginx or Apache, supporting Ruby, Node, and Python.
pros: Tight Nginx integration simplifies reverse-proxy setup
Smart spawning and dynamic worker scaling reduces idle resource usage
Enterprise edition offers advanced monitoring and rolling restarts
cons: More complex configuration, especially in a Docker-first environment
Mixed-language support (Ruby + Node) is less relevant since Mastodon's Node side is handled separately by Express
Open-source edition lacks some production features; enterprise tier adds cost
alts.when: Choose Passenger if the team already operates a Nginx-centric infrastructure and wants built-in process management without a separate process supervisor like systemd or s6.

nodes.name: Express
nodes.rationale: Express is a natural fit for Mastodon's real-time streaming layer because it excels at maintaining large numbers of persistent long-lived connections (SSE and WebSocket) without the thread-per-request overhead that Rails/Puma would incur at scale. Node.js's event-loop model handles thousands of concurrent streaming subscribers efficiently, and Express keeps the implementation lightweight while Redis pub/sub bridges events from the Rails application workers (Sidekiq) to connected clients.

alts.name: Fastify
alts.blurb: A faster, lower-overhead Node.js framework with built-in schema validation and better async ergonomics than Express.
pros: Significantly higher throughput and lower latency than Express in benchmarks
First-class async/await support reduces callback pitfalls
Built-in JSON schema validation reduces boilerplate
cons: Smaller ecosystem of middleware compared to Express
Migration from Express requires rewriting middleware and plugin structure
Team familiarity with Express may outweigh performance gains at Mastodon's scale
alts.when: Choose Fastify if streaming server CPU/memory overhead becomes a measurable bottleneck and you want to stay in the Node.js ecosystem.

alts.name: Puma + ActionCable (Rails)
alts.blurb: Consolidate streaming into the existing Rails stack using ActionCable over WebSockets, eliminating the separate Node.js service.
pros: Single unified codebase and deployment unit reduces operational complexity
Direct access to ActiveRecord, Devise sessions, and Doorkeeper tokens without HTTP re-validation
ActionCable integrates natively with Redis pub/sub
cons: Rails thread-per-connection model scales poorly for tens of thousands of simultaneous streaming clients
Puma worker memory footprint per connection is far higher than Node.js event loop
Increases load on the primary Rails app during traffic spikes
alts.when: Choose ActionCable only for smaller self-hosted instances where operational simplicity matters more than horizontal streaming scalability.

alts.name: Go (net/http or Fiber)
alts.blurb: Rewrite the streaming server in Go for maximum concurrency efficiency with goroutines handling SSE/WebSocket connections.
pros: Goroutines handle millions of concurrent connections with minimal memory per connection
Static binary simplifies Docker deployment and removes Node.js runtime dependency
Lower CPU and memory usage than Node.js at high connection counts
cons: Requires introducing a second non-Ruby/non-JS language, raising the contributor barrier
Loses the large npm ecosystem for JWT, OAuth token introspection helpers
Significant rewrite investment with limited functional gain for typical instance sizes
alts.when: Choose Go if you are operating at massive scale (hundreds of thousands of concurrent streams) and profiling proves Node.js is the bottleneck.

nodes.name: Doorkeeper
nodes.rationale: Doorkeeper is the de facto OAuth 2.0 provider gem for Rails, making it a natural fit in Mastodon's Rails/Devise stack since it integrates seamlessly with ActiveRecord (PostgreSQL) and Devise for user authentication. Mastodon exposes a public API consumed by third-party clients and other ActivityPub servers, so a mature, well-audited OAuth 2.0 server library is essential, and Doorkeeper covers all required grant flows (authorization code, client credentials) out of the box. Its tight Rails integration also means token storage, scopes, and application management fit naturally into the existing ActiveRecord/PostgreSQL data layer.

alts.name: Rodauth
alts.blurb: Full-featured Ruby authentication/authorization framework with OAuth 2.0 plugin support.
pros: More performant and secure defaults than Devise+Doorkeeper combination
Single cohesive library handles both authentication and OAuth
Fine-grained control over every auth feature
cons: Steeper learning curve; less Rails-idiomatic than Devise+Doorkeeper
Smaller ecosystem and fewer community examples for complex OAuth flows
Migrating away from Devise in an existing app is costly
alts.when: Greenfield Rails app where you want one library to own both authn and OAuth without legacy Devise dependency.

alts.name: Rack::OAuth2
alts.blurb: Low-level Rack middleware library for building OAuth 2.0 servers and clients.
pros: Lightweight with no Rails coupling, fits polyglot stacks
Full control over token storage and endpoint behavior
Works alongside Express or any Rack-compatible server
cons: No built-in admin UI or ActiveRecord models; everything must be hand-rolled
Significantly more implementation effort for grant flows and scope management
Less battle-tested at Mastodon's scale for server-side OAuth provider use
alts.when: When you need OAuth in a non-Rails or hybrid Rack/Express context and want zero framework assumptions.

alts.name: Hydra (Ory)
alts.blurb: Standalone, language-agnostic OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect server deployable as a sidecar service.
pros: Fully decoupled from the Rails app, independently scalable
OpenID Connect support out of the box
Strong security audit history and active enterprise backing
cons: Adds infrastructure complexity (another service to deploy and monitor via Docker)
Requires a consent/login app bridge to integrate with Devise user sessions
Operational overhead is disproportionate for a single-product deployment like Mastodon
alts.when: When OAuth/OIDC must serve multiple heterogeneous services and you want a dedicated identity microservice.

nodes.name: Devise
nodes.rationale: Devise is the de-facto authentication solution for Rails applications, providing a battle-tested, modular set of authentication concerns (registerable, confirmable, lockable, trackable, etc.) that integrate seamlessly with Rails and ActiveRecord/PostgreSQL. In Mastodon's stack it pairs naturally with Doorkeeper for OAuth2/OIDC token-based API access — Devise handles session-based web UI authentication while Doorkeeper handles token issuance on top of Devise's user model, a well-documented and commonly used combination. Its built-in support for email confirmation, 2FA extensions (devise-two-factor), and lockout strategies align directly with the security requirements of a federated social network.

alts.name: Rodauth
alts.blurb: A Ruby authentication framework built on Roda/Sequel or Rails, known for security-first design and database-level token management.
pros: More secure by default (database function-based token storage)
Fine-grained feature control with less metaprogramming magic
Better performance and lower overhead than Devise
cons: Smaller ecosystem and fewer community plugins
Less familiar to most Rails developers, higher onboarding cost
Doorkeeper + Rodauth integration is less documented than Doorkeeper + Devise
alts.when: Choose Rodauth when security audit requirements are strict and you want explicit, inspectable auth logic with minimal 'magic', or when starting a greenfield Rails 7+ app.

alts.name: Auth0 / Okta (managed identity provider)
alts.blurb: Fully managed authentication and identity platform accessed via OAuth2/OIDC, offloading all auth complexity to an external service.
pros: Built-in MFA, SSO, anomaly detection out of the box
Zero maintenance burden for auth infrastructure
Scales globally without in-house effort
cons: Incompatible with Mastodon's self-hosted, decentralized philosophy
Vendor lock-in and per-MAU pricing become costly at Mastodon's scale
Requires every instance operator to set up and pay for a third-party account
alts.when: Choose a managed IdP when running a centralized SaaS product with a single operator and no self-hosting requirement, and where operational simplicity outweighs cost and lock-in concerns.

alts.name: Clearance
alts.blurb: A minimal, opinionated Rails authentication gem by Thoughtbot focused on email/password authentication with a tiny, readable codebase.
pros: Extremely simple and easy to audit (~300 lines of code)
Low dependency footprint, easy to customise
Encourages ownership of auth code rather than black-box behaviour
cons: Lacks built-in modules for confirmable, lockable, trackable that Mastodon relies on
No first-class 2FA support; would require significant custom code
Much smaller community; Doorkeeper integration would need manual wiring
alts.when: Choose Clearance for small, simple apps where only basic email/password login is needed and you want maximum code transparency over feature breadth.

nodes.name: PostgreSQL
nodes.rationale: PostgreSQL is a natural fit for Mastodon's Rails-based stack because ActiveRecord has first-class PostgreSQL support, enabling use of advanced features like JSONB columns, full-text search, and array types that Mastodon actively relies on for storing ActivityPub payloads and status metadata. Its ACID compliance and robust foreign key enforcement are critical for maintaining referential integrity across Mastodon's complex social graph (accounts, statuses, follows, blocks). Combined with Chewy/Elasticsearch for search indexing and Redis for caching/queuing, PostgreSQL serves as the authoritative persistent store in a well-established Rails deployment pattern.

alts.name: MySQL / MariaDB
alts.blurb: The other dominant open-source RDBMS with solid Rails/ActiveRecord support.
pros: Widely hosted and operationally familiar
Good read performance at scale with replication
Lower memory footprint in some configurations
cons: Lacks native JSONB, array types, and some advanced index types Mastodon uses
Full-text search is weaker than PostgreSQL's built-in tsvector
Historically looser SQL standards compliance causes subtle ActiveRecord friction
alts.when: Choose if your ops team has deep MySQL expertise and you're willing to trade advanced data types for operational familiarity.

alts.name: CockroachDB
alts.blurb: PostgreSQL-wire-compatible distributed SQL database built for horizontal scale.
pros: PostgreSQL protocol means minimal ActiveRecord changes
Built-in multi-region, high-availability, and automatic sharding
Strong consistency with no single point of failure
cons: Not all PostgreSQL features (e.g. some JSONB operators, sequences) are fully supported
Higher operational complexity and cost vs. a single Postgres instance
Latency overhead from distributed consensus in single-region deployments
alts.when: Choose if you're running a very large, multi-region Mastodon instance that has outgrown vertical Postgres scaling and needs geographic distribution.

alts.name: SQLite (with Litestream / LiteFS)
alts.blurb: Lightweight embedded SQL database gaining traction in Rails with replication add-ons.
pros: Zero server process overhead, trivially simple ops for small instances
Rails 7+ has improved SQLite support; Litestream adds S3 replication
Excellent for single-server, small-community Mastodon deployments
cons: Write concurrency is severely limited — fatal for Mastodon's high write workload (Sidekiq jobs, federation ingest)
Missing many PostgreSQL features Mastodon's migrations depend on
Not viable for any medium-to-large instance without major code changes
alts.when: Choose only for a personal/single-user instance where simplicity of ops matters far more than throughput or scalability.

nodes.name: Redis
nodes.rationale: Redis is a natural fit for Mastodon's architecture because it serves dual duty as both a high-speed cache (session data, timeline fragments, rate limiting) and a message broker backing Sidekiq's background job queues, eliminating the need for a separate queuing system. Its pub/sub capabilities also power Mastodon's real-time streaming layer, which is critical for a federated social network handling bursty ActivityPub traffic. The Rails + Sidekiq combination has Redis as a de facto standard dependency, making it the lowest-friction choice in this stack.

alts.name: Valkey
alts.blurb: Open-source Redis fork maintained by the Linux Foundation after Redis Ltd.'s license change
pros: Drop-in Redis replacement with identical API and protocol
Fully open-source (BSD-3) with no commercial licensing risk
Compatible with Sidekiq, redis-rb gem, and all existing tooling
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community than Redis at this point in time
Fewer managed cloud offerings compared to Redis
alts.when: Choose Valkey when you want identical Redis semantics but need to avoid Redis Ltd.'s SSPL/RSALv2 licensing concerns in a self-hosted or open-source project.

alts.name: KeyDB
alts.blurb: Multi-threaded Redis fork offering higher throughput on multi-core hardware
pros: Drop-in Redis protocol compatibility with Sidekiq and redis-rb
Significantly higher throughput on multi-core servers due to true multi-threading
Built-in active replication simplifies HA setups
cons: Smaller community and slower security patch cadence
Multi-threaded model can introduce subtle race conditions under edge cases
Docker image maturity lags behind official Redis
alts.when: Choose KeyDB when Redis becomes a CPU bottleneck on a large Mastodon instance and you want a drop-in replacement without rewriting queue or cache logic.

alts.name: PostgreSQL (LISTEN/NOTIFY + pg_queue pattern)
alts.blurb: Use PostgreSQL's native LISTEN/NOTIFY and table-backed queuing (e.g. via GoodJob) to eliminate Redis entirely
pros: Removes an entire infrastructure dependency already present in the stack
Transactional job enqueuing prevents lost jobs on crash
GoodJob is a well-supported Sidekiq alternative with identical ActiveJob API
cons: Higher Postgres load; queuing and caching workloads compete with primary OLTP queries
No equivalent of Redis's O(1) data structures for caching timelines and rate limits
Real-time fan-out pub/sub is less performant than Redis pub/sub at Mastodon's scale
alts.when: Choose this only for small self-hosted instances where operational simplicity outweighs performance, and you want to reduce the number of running services.

nodes.name: Sidekiq
nodes.rationale: Sidekiq is a natural fit in a Rails + Redis stack like Mastodon's, leveraging Redis (already present for caching and pub/sub) as its queue backend with near-zero additional infrastructure overhead. Mastodon's ActivityPub federation requires massive volumes of async job processing — fan-out deliveries, media processing, notifications — where Sidekiq's multi-threaded, Redis-backed architecture handles high concurrency efficiently. Its deep Rails integration via ActiveJob and mature ecosystem (Sidekiq Pro, Web UI) make it the pragmatic default for a production-scale Rails application.

alts.name: GoodJob
alts.blurb: PostgreSQL-backed ActiveJob adapter that eliminates the Redis dependency entirely
pros: Uses existing PostgreSQL — one fewer infrastructure dependency
ACID-compliant job persistence with no data loss on crash
Built-in Web UI and cron scheduling
cons: Higher DB load under Mastodon-scale fan-out volumes
Slower throughput than Redis-backed queues for burst workloads
Less battle-tested at ActivityPub federation scale
alts.when: Choose GoodJob if you want to eliminate Redis and your job volume is moderate, or operational simplicity outweighs raw throughput.

alts.name: Faktory
alts.blurb: Language-agnostic job server with a dedicated worker protocol, usable from both Ruby and Node
pros: Works across Ruby and Node/Express workers in the same stack
Purpose-built job server with its own persistence layer
Better multi-language fan-out if Express workers need to enqueue jobs
cons: Adds a new stateful service (Faktory server) alongside Redis
Smaller ecosystem and fewer Rails-native conveniences than Sidekiq
Less community support and fewer production case studies at Mastodon scale
alts.when: Choose Faktory if background job processing needs to be shared across the Rails and Express layers with a unified queue, or polyglot workers become a core requirement.

alts.name: Resque
alts.blurb: Redis-backed Ruby job queue, the direct predecessor to Sidekiq in the Rails ecosystem
pros: Also uses Redis — no new infrastructure
Process-isolated workers reduce risk of thread-safety bugs
Simpler mental model for single-threaded job execution
cons: One process per worker means far higher memory usage than Sidekiq threads
Significantly lower throughput for Mastodon's federation fan-out workloads
Largely superseded by Sidekiq; slower community maintenance
alts.when: Choose Resque only if thread-safety in job code is a serious concern and the memory/throughput trade-off is acceptable — rarely the right call for new projects.

nodes.name: Chewy
nodes.rationale: Chewy is a Ruby/Rails-native Elasticsearch ORM that integrates naturally into Mastodon's Rails stack, providing a clean DSL for defining index types, bulk-import strategies, and query building without leaving the Rails paradigm. It pairs well with Sidekiq for asynchronous index updates, which Mastodon already uses heavily for background job processing, making real-time search index synchronization straightforward. For a federated social network requiring full-text search over statuses and accounts at scale, Elasticsearch backed by Chewy is a mature, battle-tested combination.

alts.name: searchkick
alts.blurb: Another Rails-to-Elasticsearch/OpenSearch ORM with a simpler API and built-in analytics helpers
pros: Slightly simpler query API with smart defaults
Native support for OpenSearch as a backend
Built-in query suggestions and typo handling
cons: Less granular control over index mappings compared to Chewy
Smaller ecosystem of advanced DSL features for complex federated queries
Fewer async/bulk reindex strategies out of the box
alts.when: Choose Searchkick if you want faster onboarding with sensible defaults and don't need fine-grained control over Elasticsearch mappings or bulk indexing pipelines.

alts.name: PostgreSQL Full-Text Search (pg_search)
alts.blurb: Leverages PostgreSQL's built-in tsvector/tsquery full-text search via the pg_search gem
pros: No additional infrastructure — PostgreSQL is already in the stack
Transactional consistency with zero index lag
Lower operational complexity and cost
cons: Scales poorly for full-text search at Mastodon's volume of federated statuses
Limited relevance tuning, no vector/semantic search, weaker multilingual support
No distributed indexing or horizontal scaling path
alts.when: Choose pg_search for smaller self-hosted instances where operational simplicity matters more than search relevance or throughput, and running Elasticsearch is impractical.

alts.name: Meilisearch
alts.blurb: A lightweight, Rust-based search engine with a simple REST API and strong developer ergonomics
pros: Very easy to self-host with minimal resource footprint
Excellent out-of-the-box relevance with typo tolerance
Growing Rails integration via meilisearch-rails gem
cons: Less mature ecosystem than Elasticsearch for complex federation-scale workloads
Limited advanced query DSL and aggregation capabilities
No native Chewy-equivalent ORM depth; Rails integration is thinner
alts.when: Choose Meilisearch for smaller or resource-constrained deployments where Elasticsearch overhead is prohibitive but better search UX than Postgres is still required.

nodes.name: Docker
nodes.rationale: Mastodon's stack is inherently multi-service — Rails/Puma, Sidekiq workers, PostgreSQL, Redis, and an Elasticsearch-backed search layer (Chewy) all need to run together coherently. Docker lets each service be defined, versioned, and spun up deterministically via docker-compose, which is critical for a federated open-source project where contributors run wildly different host environments. It also aligns naturally with the GitHub Actions CI pipeline, where containerized builds ensure parity between local dev, test, and production deployments.

alts.name: Podman
alts.blurb: Daemonless, rootless OCI-compatible container runtime that is a near drop-in replacement for Docker CLI.
pros: Rootless by default — better security posture for multi-tenant hosts
Daemonless architecture removes a single point of failure
Compatible with existing Dockerfiles and Compose files (via podman-compose or docker-compose shim)
cons: podman-compose has edge-case compatibility gaps with complex Compose configs
Smaller ecosystem of managed hosting tooling compared to Docker
Rootless networking can require extra configuration on some Linux distros
alts.when: Choose Podman when running on RHEL/Fedora-based infrastructure or when security policy mandates rootless container execution.

alts.name: Nix / NixOS
alts.blurb: Purely functional package manager and OS that produces fully reproducible dev environments and system configs without containers.
pros: Byte-for-byte reproducible builds across machines without a container runtime
Single flake.nix can replace Dockerfile, docker-compose, and parts of CI config
Atomic rollbacks of the entire environment
cons: Steep learning curve — Nix language is unfamiliar to most Rails/JS developers
Ecosystem support for Ruby gems and npm packages can lag or require manual overrides
Poor adoption among external contributors who expect docker-compose instructions
alts.when: Choose Nix when the core team prioritizes maximal reproducibility and is willing to invest in onboarding contributors to a non-Docker workflow.

alts.name: Kubernetes (k3s / k8s)
alts.blurb: Production-grade container orchestration that handles scaling, self-healing, and rolling deploys across nodes.
pros: Native horizontal scaling for Sidekiq workers and Puma — critical for large Mastodon instances
Built-in health checks, rolling updates, and secrets management
Helm charts already exist for Mastodon in the community
cons: Massive operational overhead — overkill for local dev and small/medium instances
Requires a separate local solution (minikube, k3d) to replace docker-compose in development
Steep learning curve adds friction for open-source contributors
alts.when: Choose Kubernetes when operating a large public Mastodon instance that needs autoscaling and high availability across multiple nodes.

tiers.name: Development & Testing
tiers.desc: Build tools, linters, formatters, and CI

nodes.name: Vite
nodes.rationale: Vite is a natural fit for Mastodon's React/Redux frontend because it provides near-instant HMR and ESM-native dev server performance, dramatically improving developer experience compared to older bundlers. It integrates cleanly with the Rails asset pipeline via vite_rails, aligning well with the hybrid Rails+React architecture. Its first-class Vitest compatibility also means the test toolchain is unified under one ecosystem, reducing configuration overhead.

alts.name: Webpack (via Shakapacker)
alts.blurb: The historically dominant Rails-JS bundler, used by Mastodon before its Vite migration.
pros: Mature ecosystem with deep Rails integration via Shakapacker
Extremely flexible plugin/loader system for complex transforms
Battle-tested in large production Rails apps
cons: Significantly slower cold starts and HMR vs Vite
Complex configuration that grows unwieldy at scale
Heavier memory footprint in dev
alts.when: Choose when you need highly custom build pipelines, legacy loaders, or are already deeply invested in Webpack config and migration cost outweighs DX gains.

alts.name: esbuild
alts.blurb: Extremely fast Go-based bundler used internally by Vite but also usable standalone via jsbundling-rails.
pros: Fastest raw build times available
Simple API with low configuration overhead
Native Rails integration via jsbundling-rails
cons: No HMR out of the box — requires external tooling
Limited plugin ecosystem compared to Vite/Webpack
CSS handling and code splitting less mature
alts.when: Choose when build speed is the sole priority, the frontend is relatively simple, and full HMR/plugin richness is not required.

alts.name: Rollup
alts.blurb: ESM-first bundler focused on optimal output size, well-suited for library and production builds.
pros: Produces very clean, tree-shaken output bundles
Strong ESM support and wide plugin ecosystem
Vite itself uses Rollup under the hood for production builds
cons: No built-in dev server or HMR — dev experience requires additional tooling
Slower incremental builds than Vite's dev mode
More manual setup needed for a Rails-integrated workflow
alts.when: Choose when optimizing final bundle output is critical and you are willing to compose your own dev server layer, or when building a standalone JS library rather than an app.

nodes.name: GitHub Actions
nodes.rationale: Mastodon is an open-source project hosted on GitHub, making GitHub Actions a zero-friction choice — it requires no external service integration, secrets management is built-in, and the community can submit workflow changes via standard PRs. Its native support for matrix builds suits Mastodon's multi-language stack (Ruby/Rails tested with RSpec/RuboCop, JavaScript tested with Vitest/ESLint/Stylelint) in parallel jobs without additional orchestration. Free CI minutes for public repositories also align well with an open-source project that relies on community contributions.

alts.name: CircleCI
alts.blurb: Hosted CI/CD with strong Docker layer caching and fine-grained resource class control
pros: Superior Docker build caching speeds up image-heavy pipelines
More granular compute resource classes for cost optimization
Orbs ecosystem offers reusable, well-maintained integrations
cons: Requires leaving GitHub for pipeline configuration and logs
Free tier is more restricted for open-source projects than GitHub Actions
Adds an external dependency and separate secret store to manage
alts.when: Choose CircleCI when Docker build performance is a bottleneck and the team is willing to pay for faster, more configurable runners

alts.name: GitLab CI
alts.blurb: Tightly integrated CI/CD if the project migrated to GitLab for full DevSecOps in one platform
pros: All-in-one: source control, CI, container registry, and deployments in one UI
Self-hosted runners give full control over build environment and data
Native Kubernetes and Helm deployment integrations
cons: Requires migrating away from GitHub, losing the existing contributor ecosystem and PR workflows
YAML syntax is similar but not compatible — full pipeline rewrite needed
Adds operational overhead if self-hosting the GitLab instance
alts.when: Choose GitLab CI if the project moves off GitHub or needs self-hosted infrastructure for data sovereignty

alts.name: Buildkite
alts.blurb: Hybrid CI that runs agents on your own infrastructure while Buildkite handles orchestration
pros: Agents run on self-owned hardware, enabling fast local Docker builds and caching
Scales well for large parallel test suites like Mastodon's RSpec suite
Keeps sensitive code and secrets off third-party infrastructure
cons: Requires provisioning and maintaining self-hosted build agents
Paid product with no meaningful free tier for open-source
Higher operational complexity than a fully hosted solution
alts.when: Choose Buildkite when CI speed is critical at scale and the team can afford to manage dedicated build infrastructure

nodes.name: RSpec
nodes.rationale: RSpec is the de facto standard testing framework for Rails applications and pairs naturally with Mastodon's Rails/Devise/Doorkeeper API stack, offering expressive DSL for describing complex authentication, federation, and API behaviors. Its rich ecosystem (FactoryBot, Shoulda Matchers, VCR) integrates tightly with ActiveRecord and Devise, which are central to Mastodon's architecture. The readable `describe`/`it`/`expect` syntax also aligns well with a large open-source contributor base where test clarity lowers the barrier to contribution.

alts.name: Minitest
alts.blurb: Rails' built-in, lightweight test framework with minimal DSL overhead
pros: Ships with Rails — zero extra dependencies
Faster boot time and lower memory footprint
Parallel test execution built-in
cons: Less expressive DSL makes complex federation/OAuth specs verbose
Smaller ecosystem for Rails-specific matchers compared to RSpec
Spec-style readability requires minitest-spec add-on
alts.when: Choose Minitest when minimizing dependencies and maximizing test suite speed matters more than DSL expressiveness — common in smaller or performance-sensitive Rails apps.

alts.name: Cucumber
alts.blurb: Gherkin-based acceptance testing that bridges business requirements and code
pros: Human-readable feature files ideal for documenting ActivityPub behaviors
Strong for end-to-end acceptance scenarios involving multiple actors
Encourages collaboration between non-technical stakeholders
cons: Significant overhead writing and maintaining step definitions
Slower execution; poor fit for unit/model-level tests
Rarely used alone — still needs RSpec or Minitest underneath
alts.when: Choose Cucumber when product or compliance requirements demand living documentation and acceptance tests written by non-engineers, typically layered on top of a unit framework.

alts.name: TestUnit (test-unit gem)
alts.blurb: Classic xUnit-style Ruby testing with a mature, stable API
pros: Very stable, long-lived API with few breaking changes
Familiar to developers coming from Java/Python xUnit traditions
Lightweight and compatible with older Ruby versions
cons: Verbose assertions compared to RSpec's matcher syntax
Minimal Rails-specific ecosystem (no FactoryBot-native integration)
Little adoption in modern Rails open-source projects, raising contributor friction
alts.when: Choose test-unit when maintaining legacy Ruby codebases or when team background is strongly xUnit-oriented and modern DSL frameworks are actively unwanted.

nodes.name: Vitest
nodes.rationale: Mastodon's frontend build tooling is already centered on Vite, making Vitest the natural testing companion since it shares the same config, module resolution, and transformation pipeline — eliminating the need for a separate Babel/Jest transform setup for modern ESM and TypeScript. Vitest's Jest-compatible API means the React/Redux test suite requires minimal migration effort from any prior Jest usage, and its native ESM support aligns well with a modern React codebase using Vite.

alts.name: Jest
alts.blurb: The de facto standard JavaScript test runner with the largest ecosystem and tooling support.
pros: Massive ecosystem of plugins, matchers, and community resources
Battle-tested in large React/Redux codebases at scale
Strong IDE and CI integration out of the box
cons: Requires additional Babel or ts-jest transform config to handle ESM and modern syntax used by Vite
Slower cold-start than Vitest due to separate module resolution from Vite
Duplicate config maintenance when Vite is already the build tool
alts.when: Choose Jest when the project is not using Vite, when you need maximum ecosystem compatibility, or when migrating a large legacy test suite that already targets Jest APIs.

alts.name: Mocha + Chai
alts.blurb: Flexible, minimal test framework paired with an assertion library, historically common in Node/browser projects.
pros: Highly composable — swap in any assertion or mocking library
Lightweight core with no opinions on assertions or spies
Long track record in both Node and browser environments
cons: No built-in mocking, coverage, or snapshot support — requires assembling multiple packages (Sinon, Istanbul, etc.)
ESM support has historically been inconsistent and requires extra config
No Vite integration, so cold-start and HMR benefits are lost
alts.when: Choose Mocha+Chai when you need a highly customized, piecemeal testing stack or are working in a Node-only context without a bundler like Vite.

alts.name: Jasmine
alts.blurb: Self-contained BDD test framework with built-in assertions and spies, no external dependencies required.
pros: Zero external dependencies — assertions, spies, and mocking are built in
BDD-style syntax familiar to Rails/RSpec developers already on the team
Browser-native runner available alongside Node
cons: No native Vite integration, losing config sharing and fast HMR-driven test runs
Smaller React/Redux testing ecosystem compared to Jest or Vitest
Snapshot testing and coverage require additional tooling
alts.when: Choose Jasmine when the team heavily values RSpec-style BDD consistency across the Rails and JS layers, or when running tests directly in a browser without a bundler.

nodes.name: ESLint
nodes.rationale: Mastodon's frontend is built with React, Redux, and modern JavaScript/TypeScript, making ESLint the natural choice as it has the richest ecosystem of plugins for exactly these technologies (eslint-plugin-react, eslint-plugin-react-hooks, redux-specific rules). It integrates seamlessly into the existing Vite build pipeline and GitHub Actions CI, and pairs well with the other domain-specific linters already in the stack (RuboCop for Ruby, Stylelint for CSS, Haml Lint for templates), following a clear one-linter-per-language philosophy.

alts.name: Biome
alts.blurb: Rust-based all-in-one linter and formatter replacing ESLint + Prettier with significantly faster execution
pros: Dramatically faster than ESLint on large codebases
Built-in formatting eliminates need for separate Prettier setup
Single dependency instead of ESLint + plugin ecosystem
cons: Smaller plugin ecosystem; no react-hooks or redux-specific rules yet
Migration effort and potential rule-parity gaps for an existing large codebase
Less mature and battle-tested in production monorepos
alts.when: Choose Biome when CI lint times are a bottleneck and you can accept fewer specialized React/Redux rules, especially on a greenfield project.

alts.name: oxlint
alts.blurb: Extremely fast Rust-based JavaScript/TypeScript linter designed as a drop-in ESLint complement or replacement
pros: 50-100x faster than ESLint for basic rule sets
Can run alongside ESLint to accelerate CI without full migration
Good coverage of common ESLint rules out of the box
cons: Does not support custom plugins or the full React/Redux rule set ESLint offers
Still maturing; not a full ESLint replacement for complex configurations
Adds a second linter tool to maintain if used alongside ESLint
alts.when: Choose oxlint when you want faster feedback loops in CI/pre-commit without abandoning the ESLint plugin ecosystem entirely.

alts.name: JSHint
alts.blurb: Older, simpler JavaScript linter with minimal configuration overhead
pros: Very lightweight with no complex plugin system to manage
Fast and easy to configure for basic JavaScript quality checks
Zero-dependency setup
cons: No TypeScript support, no JSX/React awareness, no hooks rules
Largely stagnant project with minimal active development
Cannot replace ESLint's rich ecosystem for a modern React + Redux stack
alts.when: Only consider JSHint for a legacy plain-JavaScript project with no framework dependencies and no TypeScript.

nodes.name: RuboCop
nodes.rationale: Mastodon is a large, multi-contributor Rails application where consistent Ruby style and catching common bugs early is critical. RuboCop is the de-facto standard Ruby static analysis tool with native Rails-aware cops (via rubocop-rails), integrates seamlessly into GitHub Actions CI alongside RSpec, and pairs naturally with the project's existing linting suite (ESLint for JS, Stylelint for CSS, Haml Lint for templates), giving a unified quality gate across all language layers.

alts.name: Standard
alts.blurb: An opinionated Ruby linter built on RuboCop that eliminates configuration decisions by enforcing a single fixed style guide.
pros: Zero configuration required — no .rubocop.yml bikeshedding
Still leverages RuboCop's engine so IDE/CI tooling works the same way
Reduces PR friction on style debates in large contributor communities
cons: No flexibility to tweak rules — all-or-nothing style adoption
Smaller ecosystem of extensions vs. the full RuboCop cop library
Migrating an existing RuboCop setup requires resolving style conflicts upfront
alts.when: Choose Standard when the team wants zero configuration overhead and is willing to fully commit to its opinionated ruleset from the start of a project.

alts.name: Reek
alts.blurb: A Ruby static analysis tool focused on detecting code smells (long methods, feature envy, etc.) rather than style enforcement.
pros: Catches deeper structural and design issues RuboCop style cops miss
Complements rather than replaces a style linter — can run alongside RuboCop
Useful for identifying refactoring targets in a large codebase like Mastodon
cons: High false-positive rate requiring significant configuration to silence noise
Does not enforce style or formatting — needs RuboCop or Standard alongside it
Less active maintenance and community momentum compared to RuboCop
alts.when: Choose Reek as a supplementary tool when the priority shifts from style consistency to architectural quality and reducing code complexity in mature codebases.

alts.name: Prism + custom rules (Shopify/syntax_tree)
alts.blurb: Syntax Tree is a Ruby formatter and AST toolkit by Shopify that provides deterministic formatting and a framework for writing custom AST-based analyses.
pros: Deterministic, opinionated formatting eliminates all style discussions
Modern AST API (Prism-backed) makes writing custom project-specific rules straightforward
Fast formatting performance suitable for large codebases
cons: Much smaller library of built-in lint rules compared to RuboCop's hundreds of cops
Younger ecosystem — fewer community plugins and less CI tooling integration
Team would need to author custom rules to replicate RuboCop's security/rails cop coverage
alts.when: Choose Syntax Tree when deterministic auto-formatting is the top priority and the team is willing to invest in writing bespoke analysis rules rather than relying on a community cop library.

nodes.name: Stylelint
nodes.rationale: Mastodon's frontend uses React with CSS/SCSS stylesheets and already employs a comprehensive linting pipeline (ESLint for JS, RuboCop for Ruby, Haml Lint for templates), making Stylelint the natural complement to enforce consistent CSS/SCSS conventions across the codebase. It integrates seamlessly into the Vite-based build toolchain and GitHub Actions CI pipeline, and its extensive plugin ecosystem supports SCSS syntax which Mastodon relies on heavily. As a de facto industry standard for CSS linting, it reduces onboarding friction for open-source contributors already familiar with it.

alts.name: ESLint with eslint-plugin-css
alts.blurb: Leverage the existing ESLint setup to also lint CSS-in-JS or plain CSS without adding a separate tool.
pros: Single linter process and config for JS and CSS reduces tooling overhead
Already integrated into CI and editor configs
Fewer dependencies to maintain
cons: CSS-in-JS coverage is narrow; poor support for standalone .scss files Mastodon uses
Plugin ecosystem is immature compared to Stylelint
Less granular SCSS-specific rules
alts.when: Choose this if the project migrates to CSS-in-JS (e.g., styled-components) and wants to consolidate linting under one tool.

alts.name: Prettier (CSS formatting)
alts.blurb: Opinionated formatter that auto-fixes CSS/SCSS style issues rather than reporting them.
pros: Zero-config formatting removes style debates entirely
Already potentially used alongside ESLint in the JS pipeline
Fast and widely adopted in open-source projects
cons: Formatter only — does not catch logical CSS errors or enforce best practices like Stylelint does
No rule customization; can conflict with existing SCSS conventions
Requires pairing with a linter for full coverage
alts.when: Choose this as a complement (not replacement) when the team wants auto-formatting over manual rule enforcement, ideally used alongside a lighter linter.

alts.name: PostCSS with custom plugins
alts.blurb: Use PostCSS's plugin pipeline to enforce project-specific CSS rules as part of the build transform step.
pros: Already likely in the Vite pipeline; no extra tooling layer
Highly customizable for Mastodon-specific conventions
Can enforce rules and transform CSS simultaneously
cons: No off-the-shelf rule library; custom plugins require significant maintenance
Poor developer feedback loop compared to dedicated linter IDE integration
Overkill and fragile for what is essentially a solved problem
alts.when: Choose this only if Mastodon needed deeply custom CSS transformations and linting tied together in one step, which is unlikely for a standard SCSS codebase.

nodes.name: Haml Lint
nodes.rationale: Mastodon's Rails layer uses HAML for server-rendered views (emails, error pages, fallback HTML), making Haml Lint a natural fit alongside the existing RuboCop/ESLint/Stylelint suite to enforce consistent style and catch syntax errors in those templates. It integrates cleanly into the GitHub Actions CI pipeline and follows the same 'a linter per language/template type' philosophy already established in the stack. Since HAML is the chosen templating language for the Rails side, a HAML-specific linter is the most targeted and low-overhead way to maintain template quality.

alts.name: ERB Lint
alts.blurb: Linter for ERB templates, the default Rails templating format, with pluggable rules and RuboCop integration.
pros: ERB is Rails default so tooling ecosystem is larger
Tight RuboCop integration for Ruby-in-template checks
No additional template language dependency needed
cons: Only relevant if templates are migrated from HAML to ERB
ERB templates are more verbose and less structured than HAML
Switching templating languages is a significant refactor cost
alts.when: Choose this if the team decides to migrate away from HAML to standard ERB to reduce the number of non-default Rails dependencies.

alts.name: RuboCop (with custom cops)
alts.blurb: Extend the already-present RuboCop setup with custom or community cops that partially cover template hygiene concerns.
pros: Already in the stack — zero new tooling to install or configure
Unified reporting surface for all Ruby-adjacent code
Active community of extension gems
cons: Cannot parse or understand HAML syntax natively
Template-specific rules (indentation, attribute formatting) are not expressible
Would leave HAML files entirely unlinted structurally
alts.when: Choose this only if HAML usage is minimal and the team wants to consolidate tooling rather than achieve deep template linting coverage.

alts.name: Slim-Lint
alts.blurb: Equivalent linter for Slim templates, another concise Ruby-friendly HTML templating language.
pros: Very similar developer experience and rule structure to Haml Lint
Slim templates are arguably more concise and performant than HAML
Same CI integration pattern as Haml Lint
cons: Requires migrating all existing HAML templates to Slim
Slim has a smaller community and fewer resources than HAML
Non-trivial migration cost with no clear functional benefit for Mastodon's use case
alts.when: Choose this only if starting a greenfield Rails project and preferring Slim over HAML; not a practical swap for an existing HAML codebase.
